articles.title: Trump scolds Netanyahu over Lebanon strikes at G7, calls deal with Iran 'not final'
articles.summary: President Trump said he was unhappy with PM Netanyahu's handling of Israel's 'minor war' against Hezbollah, saying Netanyahu 'gets a little excited sometimes' and 'has to be more responsible with respect to Lebanon,' calling last weekend's Beirut-area strikes 'vicious' and 'too much.' Trump said the US-Iran memorandum of understanding is 'not final' and warned Washington could resume strikes on Iran if 'they don't behave,' while insisting the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz was unavoidable. The friction underscores how the parallel Lebanon conflict is complicating efforts to close out the Iran deal.

articles.title: Israeli air strikes on Lebanon continue, threatening US-Iran deal
articles.summary: Israeli forces attempted to capture Ali al-Taher, a strategic hill southeast of Nabatieh held by Hezbollah, and carried out air strikes on the outskirts of Kfar Tebnit and raids on Nabatieh al-Fawqa. The Israeli army said an explosive Hezbollah drone detonated near its troops in southern Lebanon, wounding four soldiers. The continued fighting is seen as one of the biggest threats to the framework agreement ending the US-Israel war on Iran, with Tehran warning that fresh strikes on Lebanon or continued occupation would be treated as a violation of the deal.

articles.title: Iran says US deal requires Israeli forces to leave Lebanon; Israel says troops will stay
articles.summary: Iran's foreign minister said any Israeli forces remaining in southern Lebanon, or any Israeli strikes there, would constitute a violation of the US-Iran agreement due to be signed Friday. Israeli officials pushed back, saying troops would remain in Lebanon because 'Trump's agreement does not bind us.' The dispute over Lebanon has emerged as the central sticking point as the parties move toward the formal signing.

articles.title: Oil industry makes tentative moves ahead of deal to reopen Strait of Hormuz
articles.summary: With Friday's signing approaching, a handful of ships have begun rerouting toward the Gulf and Iranian tankers laden with crude are moving out, the first tentative signs of a restart after months of de facto blockade. Under leaked terms of the interim accord, Iran will reopen the strait and be allowed to sell oil without restrictions, but full resumption will be gradual: the deal targets prewar traffic in 30 days, while mine-clearing could take up to six months and restoring full production several more.

articles.title: Switzerland confirms US-Iran deal will be signed at Burgenstock resort Friday
articles.summary: Switzerland confirmed the memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran will be signed Friday, June 19, at the Burgenstock resort near Lucerne, a site chosen because it is difficult to access and easily secured. Bern is acting as facilitator, providing diplomatic and logistical arrangements, with the venue proposed by Pakistani and Qatari mediators. Trump and Vice President Vance signed the text virtually on June 14 ahead of the in-person ceremony.

articles.title: Israel approves settler building plans in West Bank city of Hebron
articles.summary: Israel approved the expansion of a Jewish school for settlers in the centre of Hebron, a construction push Palestinians say violates the 1997 Hebron Agreement. Israel's finance minister announced the plans a day after saying he had scrapped a deal giving the Palestinian municipality control over certain planning around Hebron's historic core. Critics call it part of a broader move toward de facto annexation.

articles.title: Settlers set fire to West Bank mosque in Jaljilia amid arson spree
articles.summary: Palestinians inspected damage at a mosque in Jaljilia near Ramallah after it was reportedly set on fire by settlers, part of a wider wave of attacks that included an attempt to burn a mosque with worshippers inside and a separate arson in Deir Dibwan where vehicles were torched. The EU's top diplomat has warned of an 'alarming' surge in settler violence across the occupied West Bank.

articles.title: More than 1,000 Palestinians killed in Gaza since ceasefire, authorities say
articles.summary: Palestinian authorities said more than 1,000 people have been killed in Gaza since the October 10 ceasefire took effect. The UN Security Council met to urge consolidation of the tenuous truce and warned of escalating violence and settlement activity in the West Bank, a day after the body of the last Israeli hostage in Gaza was retrieved. Negotiations over a second phase remain stalled, with Hamas disarmament the main sticking point.

articles.title: Houthis ban Israeli navigation in Red Sea as tankers attacked off Yemen
articles.summary: The UK Maritime Trade Operations agency reported two incidents off Yemen on June 17: a tanker about 111 nautical miles southeast of Aden was fired on with an RPG by an armed skiff, and a container vessel 14 nautical miles off the coast came under fire in an attempted boarding. The Houthis said they launched missiles at targets in the Tel Aviv-Jaffa area and declared a complete ban on Israeli-linked maritime movements in the Red Sea, framing it as part of the 'Axis of Resistance.'

articles.title: Riyadh Air wins US approval to fly between Saudi Arabia and the United States
articles.summary: Saudi Arabia's new flag carrier Riyadh Air received approval from the US Department of Transportation to operate flights between the kingdom and the United States, a milestone for the airline's launch plans. Separately, Fitch reported that GCC investment-grade debt spreads have returned to pre-war levels, a sign of normalising regional risk sentiment as the Iran war winds down.

articles.title: Iran exports oil for first time in two months as ceasefire holds
articles.summary: Iran resumed crude oil exports for the first time in roughly two months as the fragile ceasefire held and the parties prepared to sign the interim deal, under which Tehran will be permitted to sell its oil without restrictions. The resumption is an early economic dividend of the agreement, though officials caution that returning shipping through the Strait of Hormuz to prewar levels will take weeks to months.
